front: Ruler: commissioned/ordered the transformation of the Palace of Versailles from a hunting lodge into a grand royal residence
Ruler: declared (possibly apocryphally) "I am the state" (L'état, c'est moi)
Ruler: nicknamed the "Sun King" (le Roi Soleil); depictions of his face often carry the motto "Nec pluribus impar"
Ruler: reigned for 72 years, the longest reign of any monarch in history
Minister: succeeded the "Red Eminence" as chief minister of France (1642-1661)
work: The Fronde and the Regency of Cardinal Mazarin

front: Minister: tutored the young Louis XIV and signed the Peace of Westphalia while governing as chief minister during his regency
work: The Fronde and the Regency of Cardinal Mazarin

front: Event: mid-17th-century French civil war of noble/parlementaire revolt named for the slingshots used by rioting Parisian crowds; its suppression became an early model for absolutist governance
work: The Fronde and the Regency of Cardinal Mazarin

front: Battle: fought during the Fronde, a rebellion started by the appellate courts called parlements
back: Battle of the Faubourg St. Antoine
work: The Fronde and the Regency of Cardinal Mazarin

front: War: ended by the 1648 Treaty of Westphalia; France's victory at the Battle of Rocroi in this war crushed Spanish military dominance, setting the stage for the Fronde
work: The Fronde and the Regency of Cardinal Mazarin

front: Institution: officials temporarily appointed from the nobles of the robe to enforce royal power in the provinces, part of Louis XIV's centralizing administration after the Fronde
work: The Fronde and the Regency of Cardinal Mazarin

front: Treaty: signed in 1659 by Louis XIV and Philip IV of Spain, ending a war that included the Battle of the Dunes
War: sparked by the unpaid dowry from Louis XIV's marriage to Maria Theresa of Spain, following the death of her father Philip IV
War: fought by Louis XIV after he invaded the United Provinces, having first signed the Treaty of Dover with England
Treaty: signed in 1678 and 1679, ending the Franco-Dutch War and giving France significant holdings in modern Belgium

War: fought by Louis XIV against a coalition of the Holy Roman Empire, Spain, and Britain (led by William III) resisting his territorial expansion; his engineer Vauban captured and later defended Namur during it
back: War of the League of Augsburg (War of the Grand Alliance / Nine Years' War)

front: Ruler: placed his grandson Philip of Anjou on the throne of Spain as Philip V after the death of Charles II, sparking a war fought over the succession

Treaty: peace negotiations here, ending the War of the Spanish Succession, forced the House of Bourbon to grant Britain the asiento slave-trade monopoly and split the dynasty into separate French and Spanish lines
Document: this 1685 edict revoked the Edict of Nantes, ending religious toleration and driving most French Huguenots to flee
Policy: forced religious minorities (Huguenots) to quarter destructive, unruly soldiers, pressuring them to convert
Event: Louis XIV razed this Jansenist stronghold abbey
Event: sparked when Louis XIV requested the apostolic constitution Regiminis Apostolici from Pope Alexander VII, requiring clergy to subscribe to a "formula for submission"
Document: this 1682 declaration, welcomed by Antoine Arnauld, codified Gallicanism and caused a long feud between Louis XIV and the papacy
back: Declaration of the Clergy of France

front: Concept: anti-ultramontanist doctrine championed by Pierre Pithou holding that the Pope had spiritual, but not temporal, jurisdiction; codified in Louis XIV's Declaration of the Clergy of France
Event: privileges created by Louis XIV at a clergy assembly, then annulled by Pope Innocent XI, who also vetoed Louis's choice for Prince-elector of Cologne
Ruler: feuded with Pope Innocent XI over Gallican church privileges, though he found some detente with Innocent XI's successor, Innocent XII
Concept: mystical doctrine that the mystic Jeanne Guyon convinced Louis XIV's secret wife to adopt, and for advancing which Jacques-Bénigne Bossuet was persecuted; condemned by Pope Innocent XII
Ruler: his nephew Victor Amadeus II of Savoy massacred the Waldensians, a persecuted Christian sect
Ruler: during his reign, the Camisards (a Protestant rebel group), led by Abraham Mazel, assassinated François Langlade
Document: 1685 decree issued by Louis XIV and written by Colbert and the Marquis de Seignelay, regulating slavery in the French Empire, including a requirement that all slaves be baptized Catholic
Minister: rose from intendant to finance minister under Louis XIV, basing economic policy around the accumulation of gold
work: Ministers and Servants of the Crown: Colbert, Fouquet, and Vauban

front: Minister: as Superintendent of Buildings, maintained the Maison du Roi for Louis XIV
work: Ministers and Servants of the Crown: Colbert, Fouquet, and Vauban

front: Minister: ordered construction of the Canal du Midi, connecting Toulouse to the Mediterranean
work: Ministers and Servants of the Crown: Colbert, Fouquet, and Vauban

front: Minister: this earlier finance minister to Louis XIV fell from favor after throwing a lavish party at his chateau of Vaux-le-Vicomte
work: Ministers and Servants of the Crown: Colbert, Fouquet, and Vauban

front: Minister: died serving a prison term at the fortress of Pignerol
work: Ministers and Servants of the Crown: Colbert, Fouquet, and Vauban

front: Figure: developed the technique of ricochet firing, in which projectiles were launched at lower angles and lower power
work: Ministers and Servants of the Crown: Colbert, Fouquet, and Vauban

front: Figure: Louis XIV's military engineer, who designed and besieged star-shaped fortresses, including the Citadel of Lille, captured and fortified Namur against Dutch counterpart Menno van Coehoorn, and expanded Luxembourg's Bock fortress
work: Ministers and Servants of the Crown: Colbert, Fouquet, and Vauban

front: Artist: named "first painter" by Louis XIV, decorating the Hall of Mirrors, the Ambassador Staircase, and the Salons of War and Peace; Louis called him the "greatest French painter of all time"
Ruler: had an eponymous artistic style with three periods, influenced by painter Nicolas Poussin
Painting: commissioned by Louis XIV, depicts a tent scene where Sisygambis mistakenly kneels to Hephaestion instead of Alexander the Great
back: The Family of Darius Before Alexander (Charles Le Brun)

front: Ruler: as patron of the Academy, oversaw the formulation of a "hierarchy of genres" placing history painting at the top and still life at the bottom
Architect: chief architect to Louis XIV, designed the Place Vendôme, the Hall of Mirrors, the Grand Trianon, and the Orangerie
Architect: worked with Cardinal Mazarin and contributed to Louis XIV's preferred classicist style; his grand-nephew Jules Hardouin later adopted this architect's surname
Artist: landscaped the Versailles gardens as the palace was expanded under Louis XIV
Ruler: rejected a palace facade design from Gian Lorenzo Bernini in favor of a colonnade by Claude Perrault

Sculpture: an unflattering bust/statue of Louis XIV by Gian Lorenzo Bernini was balanced by this bronze equestrian statue by François Girardon (later modified into Marcus Curtius) in the Place Vendôme
back: Equestrian statue of Louis XIV (François Girardon)

front: Painting: full-length "swagger" portrait of Louis XIV in a blue robe embroidered with gold fleurs-de-lis, holding a scepter, with a sword hilt visible behind him
Painting: depicts a worker boxing away a portrait of Louis XIV as a woman in pink looks on, at an art boutique
Ruler: brought the Gobelins tapestry factory to prominence
Ruler: depicted in a Versailles ceiling painting standing opposite Glory (seated on a cloud) and above two putti playing cards
Ruler: depicted in a Versailles ceiling painting carrying a golden tiller while gazing at his reflection in Minerva's shield
Ruler: depicted in a portrait standing on a floral, golden carpet beneath a gold-engraved figure of Justice
Ruler: danced the role of Apollo in the Ballet Royal de la Nuit, a self-presentation that inspired his "Sun King" nickname
Composer: served as Louis XIV's royal music master/court composer for most of his career
Composer: pioneered the tragédie en musique genre (beginning with the opera Cadmus et Hermione) and the French overture
Composer: wrote incidental music for Molière plays such as Le Bourgeois Gentilhomme and The Pleasures of the Enchanted Isle
Opera: known as "the king's opera" for seemingly depicting Louis XIV's real-life love triangle with the queen and Madame de Maintenon
Ruler: an extravagant revival of one of his court composer's operas, with a 95-voice choir and 80 dancers, helped inaugurate an opera hall that he left unfinished
Composer: while premiering his Te Deum as Louis XIV's Master of Music, contracted gangrene after stabbing himself with a conducting staff
Composer: nicknamed "le Grand"; consecrated Louis XIV's chapel organ and was later named the king's court harpsichordist
Composer: protected by the Duchess of Guise, flouted Lully's monopoly on theater music by writing music for Molière's The Imaginary Invalid
Ruler: befriended his court composer while dancing together in the Ballet Royal de la Nuit; that composer later wrote a grand motet ironically celebrating his recovery from surgery
Ruler: banished his first love, Mazarin's niece Anna Maria Mancini, from court so he could marry Maria Theresa of Spain instead
work: Mistresses, Marriage, and the Affair of the Poisons

front: Ruler: had an early love affair with Françoise Scarron, later known as Madame de Maintenon
work: Mistresses, Marriage, and the Affair of the Poisons

front: Event: sensational scandal in which fortune-teller La Voisin confessed to selling illicit poisons and was said to have murdered thousands of infants; a tribunal under Nicolas de la Reynie sentenced 36 people to death, and Louis XIV's mistress Madame de Montespan was implicated
work: Mistresses, Marriage, and the Affair of the Poisons

front: Event: Mazarin's niece Olympia Mancini, an influential mistress of Louis XIV, was expelled from court during this scandal for plotting with the fortune-teller La Voisin
work: Mistresses, Marriage, and the Affair of the Poisons

front: Ruler: secretly married this woman as his second wife
work: Mistresses, Marriage, and the Affair of the Poisons

front: Institution: founded by Madame de Maintenon, Louis XIV's secret second wife, to offer educational opportunities to noble girls
work: Mistresses, Marriage, and the Affair of the Poisons

front: Figure: term for Louis XIV's children born to mistresses such as Louise de La Vallière and Madame de Montespan
Figure: illegitimate son of Louis XIV and Madame de Montespan who became Grand Admiral of France at the age of 5
Figure: illegitimate daughter of Louis XIV and Louise de La Vallière
Figure: illegitimate son of Louis XIV and Madame de Montespan who married Louise Bénédicte; his correspondence with Giulio Alberoni was part of the Cellamare conspiracy to replace the Regency
Figure: married his first cousin, an illegitimate daughter of Louis XIV and sister of the Duke of Maine, becoming his wife as Regent of France
back: Philippe II, Duke of Orléans (the Regent)

front: Ruler: the marriages of his illegitimate children to Princes of the Blood caused controversy at his court
